authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR Part 259 - Enhanced Protections for Airline Passengers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOT aviation consumer-protection regulations establish required airline customer service plan, tarmac-delay, baggage, cancellation, and notification commitments that directly govern passenger-facing airport service responses.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: passenger service intake, delay and cancellation communication, baggage-status communication, and customer-service plan boundary setting
used_for: refund, cancellation, baggage, and tarmac-delay escalation triggers

authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR Part 260 - Refunds and Other Consumer Protections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOT refund rules are authoritative for air-travel refund-trigger and timing questions, so airport customer service can state documented status but must not approve refunds outside carrier authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: refund-status explanation, compensation boundary handling, and policy-exception escalation

authoritative_sources.title: 14 CFR Part 382 - Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Air Travel
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOT disability regulations define airline accessibility service and complaint-resolution obligations, including boundaries where trained carrier roles such as Complaint Resolution Officials are required.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: accessibility-service intake, special-service request review, mobility-device handling, and complaint escalation

authoritative_sources.title: 19 CFR Part 122 - Air Commerce Regulations
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Customs and Border Protection / eCFR
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP air commerce regulations define air manifest, advance cargo information, permit-to-unlade or lade, and customs-control requirements that gate compliant air cargo and international aircraft movement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cargo documentation review, customs-hold communication, release-boundary handling, and documentation-gap escalation
used_for: airway bill and manifest evidence expectations

authoritative_sources.title: What Can I Bring?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TSA is the federal transportation security authority, and its passenger screening guidance establishes the security-screening boundary that airport customer service cannot override.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: passenger security-sensitive request triage, prohibited-item communication, and security-owner handoff
used_for: airport security or TSA escalation posture

authoritative_sources.title: Traffic Flow Management in the National Airspace System
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FAA traffic-flow management guidance is authoritative for EDCT, ground delay program, reroute, and airspace constraint terminology that affects airport disruption communications but remains outside customer-service authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: route or schedule disruption review, movement-status communication, and dispatch-boundary escalation

authoritative_sources.title: IATA Resolution 753 - Baggage Tracking
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA is the airline standards body, and Resolution 753 defines baggage tracking points at acceptance, loading, transfer, and delivery that customer service uses to identify custody breaks without inventing liability outcomes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: baggage status review, transfer-gap detection, mishandled-baggage escalation, and custody evidence mapping
used_for: bag tag, BSM, BRS, transfer, and delivery evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Airport Handling Manual
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA airport handling standards are a recognized global operating baseline for passenger, baggage, cargo, ramp, and station handling practices that shape service-level and handoff expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: station service handling, baggage and cargo handoff review, operating terminology, and service-level escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Annex 9 - Facilitation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ICAO Annex 9 is the international facilitation baseline for airport passengers, cargo, customs, immigration, health, operator, airport, and customer formalities, clarifying the multi-authority nature of airport service work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: international passenger and cargo facilitation, border-formality boundary setting, and authority handoff control
used_for: international passenger and cargo customer context

authoritative_sources.title: Airport Service Quality Program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ACI is the global airport trade association, and ASQ is a recognized airport customer-experience and service-quality reference for airport-facing service-level expectations and improvement language.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: customer-experience service-quality framing, complaint triage, and service recovery evidence capture

workflow_stages.name: Service intake and authority grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm passenger, shipper, consignee, airline, station, flight, baggage, cargo, accessibility, or disruption context; classify the request; and verify whether customer service owns the response or must route to another authority.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Record and custody review
workflow_stages.description: Validate PNR, ticket, DCS, bag tag, BSM, BRS, PIR, airway bill, house airway bill, ULD, load, warehouse, route, customs, and service-log records so the response is tied to authoritative status.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance

workflow_stages.name: Supported resolution and stakeholder communication
workflow_stages.description: Prepare evidence-backed resolution notes and passenger, shipper, consignee, airline, or airport communication summaries that state what is known, what was done, what remains blocked, and who owns the next step.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Exception, complaint, and repeat-failure escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate unsupported compensation, refund, service recovery, accessibility complaint, repeated baggage failure, cargo release, or policy-conflict cases with the correct owner and evidence set.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Movement, route, customs, security, and safety stop-condition handling
workflow_stages.description: Stop unsupported action and escalate whenever movement depends on dispatch, customs release, TSA/security screening, maintenance release, dangerous goods or cargo safety, accessibility complaint authority, or other regulated approval.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references

workflow_stages.name: Handoff, closure, and tenant adaptation control
workflow_stages.description: Confirm what counts as resolved versus handed off, record the next owner, preserve the public baseline, and apply tenant-specific airport or carrier rules only when retrieved and traceable.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

artifacts: PNR, ticket number, ticket coupon status, boarding pass, itinerary, flight number, airport, station, terminal, gate, and customer contact history
DCS check-in events, service log, CRM case, station notes, complaint records, customer communication history, and carrier policy extracts
bag tag, BSM, BRS, transfer record, load scan, arrival scan, mishandled baggage or PIR record, delivery record, and baggage service recovery notes
airway bill, house airway bill, cargo booking, ULD identifier, load plan, acceptance record, warehouse handoff, release order, customs status, and compliance documents
route, schedule, EDCT, ground delay program, diversion, cancellation, connection, gate, stand, and dispatch status records
accessibility assistance request, SSR, CRO escalation record, mobility device record, service animal documentation status, and disability complaint record
TSA or airport security routing notes, customs hold notes, dangerous-goods or cargo safety flags, maintenance blockers, and escalation records
systems_of_record: cargo booking and airway bill system
accessibility service request or CRO case system

allowed: Classify requests as in scope, out of scope, blocked, informational, complaint-driven, exception-driven, or owner-transfer sensitive.
Request minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Provide evidence-backed passenger, shipper, consignee, airline, airport, or stakeholder status and next-step summaries without overstating authority.
Prepare resolution notes, customer communication summaries, handoff summaries, and escalation packages when ownership shifts.
Coordinate communication around baggage, cargo, route, and handoff status while preserving chain-of-custody and movement-approval boundaries.
escalate: Required PNR, ticket, DCS, baggage, cargo, route, dispatch, customs, warehouse, accessibility, security, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
The request requires dispatch, customs clearance, TSA or airport security, maintenance-release, safety, legal, refund, compensation, accessibility complaint, or policy-override authority.
A custody break, customs hold, security screening issue, EDCT or route disruption, mobility-device issue, dangerous-goods flag, or safety-sensitive movement dependency changes the safe answer.
Public baseline guidance and tenant, carrier, airport, TSA, CBP, IATA, jurisdiction, or station policy materially conflict.
refuse: Promise boarding, carriage, cargo release, baggage delivery, refund, compensation, or service recovery that the authoritative record does not support.
Override carrier, airport, TSA, CBP, customs, security, dispatch, maintenance, accessibility, refund, or compensation controls without explicit authority.
Authorize prohibited-item carriage, customs clearance, permit to unlade, dangerous-goods movement, dispatch decision, load-control action, warehouse release, or safety workaround.

domain_failure_modes: the lane promises compensation, refund, boarding, carriage, baggage delivery, or cargo release when records and authority do not support it
a baggage or cargo chain-of-custody break is treated as routine customer service instead of a documented escalation
customer service implies customs clearance, TSA/security exception, dispatch, load-control, maintenance-release, or safety authority
a route or EDCT disruption is communicated as a guaranteed schedule outcome without dispatch or air traffic authority
an accessibility complaint or mobility-device issue is handled without the required trained owner or complaint-resolution pathway
system-of-record conflicts across PNR, DCS, baggage, cargo, warehouse, customs, CRM, and service logs are hidden from the escalation package
the industry label is cosmetic and the pack collapses into generic customer support instead of airport passenger, baggage, cargo, movement, and regulated-boundary handling

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Route or schedule disruption with compliance impact
Request that implies customs, dispatch, or safety authority
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Carrier contracts of carriage, airport tenant rules, local station procedures, jurisdiction-specific passenger-rights rules, refund or compensation authority, baggage claim limits, cargo tariffs, and accessibility escalation paths can materially narrow the public baseline.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This pack uses DOT, CBP, TSA, FAA, IATA, ICAO, and ACI public sources as the baseline. Runtime must retrieve tenant, carrier, airport, station, jurisdiction, and program rules before applying a tenant-specific outcome.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Different countries and airport authorities assign customs, immigration, security, and accessibility responsibilities differently.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The package treats this as an explicit routing and retrieval dependency rather than guessing. validated use is safe because any jurisdiction-sensitive answer is blocked or escalated until the relevant tenant and authority context is available.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, movement/custody records, regulated approval boundaries, and failure behavior rather than generic customer-service scripting.

The lane stops at customer-service resolution and communication. Dispatch, customs clearance, security screening, maintenance release, safety approval, legal interpretation, final refund or compensation approval, and formal accessibility complaint authority remain outside the lane.

No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package because unresolved jurisdiction and tenant differences are represented as required retrieval, refusal, or escalation conditions.
